coredata.dc:title: Detection of Cadmium-related ions by MALDI TOF mass spectrometry correlates with physicochemical properties of Cadmium/matrix adducts
coredata.dc:creator: Nešić, Maja D.
Algarra, Manuel
Soto, Juan
Nenadović, Miloš
Popović, Iva
Petković, Marijana
coredata.dc:description: Metal acetate complexes are highly interesting in designing and synthesizing metal–organic frameworks, which have potential applications in gas storage, molecular sieves, ion exchange, and catalysis. Cadmium is well recognized as an industrial pollutant and a food contaminant that severely affects human health. For these reasons, the detection and analysis of Cd are of great interest. A major challenge in studying cadmium compounds' structure is that Cd2+ can have a wide range of coordination numbers and diverse geometries. (MA)LDI is extremely useful for detecting Cd(OAc)2·2H2O and investigating Cd's complex and flexible nature. We applied LDI to detect and characterize the Cd-related clusters and MALDI approach to investigate the Cd(OAc)2x2H2O reactivity and detectability with the five different organic matrices. The results revealed that in the solid-state Cd(OAc)2x2H2O tend to form coordination polymers, and in the presence of coordinating ligands (matrix molecules) form smaller complexes. In addition, the correlation between Cd-related LOD and matrix features was observed and discussed, indicating that the LODs of this analyte strongly depend on the optical absorption of the matrix-analyte adduct.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cadmium Acetate
Matrix
MALDI TOF MS
Ellipsometry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of samples for mass spectrometry
Determination of the limit of detection (LOD)
LDI TOF mass spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O
MALDI TOF MS spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O
LODs of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O in the tested matrices
Correlation of physicochemical properties of matrices with LOD of Cd-ions
Ionization potential, pKa, and mp of matrices and LOD
Effect of the matrix optical properties on the Cd(OAc)2·2H2O LODs studied by SE

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the Serbian Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development (451-03-68/2020-14/200017), Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades , Spain (RTI2018-099668-BC22 project). Experiments at MALDI TOF MS were performed at CQM-Madeira Chemistry Research Centre, University of Madeira , Funchal, Portugal, by M. Petković and authors are thankful to FCT-Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (CQM Base Fund - UIDB/00674/2020, ARDITI-CQM_2019-018-ISG (MP), project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-Centro de Química da Madeira-CQM+(Madeira 14-20).

This work has been supported by the Serbian Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development (451-03-68/2020-14/200017), Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades, Spain (RTI2018-099668-BC22 project). Experiments at MALDI TOF MS were performed at CQM-Madeira Chemistry Research Centre, University of Madeira, Funchal, Portugal, by M. Petković and authors are thankful to FCT-Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (CQM Base Fund - UIDB/00674/2020, ARDITI-CQM_2019-018-ISG (MP), project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-Centro de Química da Madeira-CQM+(Madeira 14-20).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Negative ion LDI TOF mass spectra of 0.5 mg/mL Cd(OAc)2·2H2O. (b) SE solid phase spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O.

Isotopic distribution of Cd(OAc)3 − ion acquired in LDI mode (bottom trace) (0.5 mg/mL). The upper spectrum is a theoretical presentation of the signals (Selket software). Relative abundances of isotopes in the group Cd(OAc)3 − acquired signals (LDI) are presented in Table 2 (insert in Fig. 2).

The negative ion MALDI mass spectra of 0.5 mg/mL Cd(OAc)2·2H2O acquired with (a) DHB, (b) CHCA, (c) 9-AA, (d) AT and (e) F20TPP. An asterisk indicates matrix signals. SE solid phase spectra of matrices (black lines) and corresponding spectra of the matrices after mixing with Cd(OAc)2·2H2O are given by the red lines (f-j). ((Colour online.))

Comparison of matrix features (pKa, IP, and RI) and corresponding LODs of the analyte in the four different matrices (a) and the plot of the mp of matrices vs. LODs (b). LOD in AT matrix is not presented because it was impossible to detect the analyte ions in this matrix. Instead, features of the matrices are given in Supp Info.

Comparison of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O LODs in the tested matrices and their corresponding km355 and kma355 values. Table 5. k m355 and k ma355 values obtained by SE for 5 matrices, namely DHB, CHCA, AT, 9AA, and F20TPP.
Position and identity of signals detectable in the negative ion mode LDI TOF mass spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O.

Position and identity of signals detectable in the negative ion mode MALDI TOF mass spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O acquired with the indicated matrices. An asterisk indicates all signals arising from the organic matrices. Signals are indicated by the detected experimentally acquired position of the most intense signal in the group that belongs to the ion.

IP, and VE energies of the most abundant ions detected in the mass spectra are given in square brackets in eV. They correspond to the absorption maximums given above in nm. Ions with the highest intensity in each mass spectrum are emboldened.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aM05-2X(D3)/def2-SVPP. bOscillator strength. cOrder of the excited state corresponding to the tabulated vertical excitation.

head.ce:title: Detection of Cadmium-related ions by MALDI TOF mass spectrometry correlates with physicochemical properties of Cadmium/matrix adducts

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Thin Films And Integrated Micro/nanosystems, Department of Atomic Physics, Vinča Institute of Nuclear Sciences-National Institute of the Republic of Serbia, University of Belgrade, Mike Petrovića Alasa 12-14, 11351 Belgrade, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work studied the relationship between the structure and the detectability of Cd-related clusters generated from Cd(OAc)2·2H2O by matrix-assisted and matrix-free laser irradiation mass spectrometry and the compound's physicochemical properties. The limit of detection of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O depends on the optical absorption of the matrix-analyte adducts.

Metal acetate complexes are highly interesting in designing and synthesizing metal–organic frameworks, which have potential applications in gas storage, molecular sieves, ion exchange, and catalysis. Cadmium is well recognized as an industrial pollutant and a food contaminant that severely affects human health. For these reasons, the detection and analysis of Cd are of great interest. A major challenge in studying cadmium compounds' structure is that Cd2+ can have a wide range of coordination numbers and diverse geometries. (MA)LDI is extremely useful for detecting Cd(OAc)2·2H2O and investigating Cd's complex and flexible nature. We applied LDI to detect and characterize the Cd-related clusters and MALDI approach to investigate the Cd(OAc)2x2H2O reactivity and detectability with the five different organic matrices. The results revealed that in the solid-state Cd(OAc)2x2H2O tend to form coordination polymers, and in the presence of coordinating ligands (matrix molecules) form smaller complexes. In addition, the correlation between Cd-related LOD and matrix features was observed and discussed, indicating that the LODs of this analyte strongly depend on the optical absorption of the matrix-analyte adduct.

ce:section.ce:para: Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI MS) is a surface sampling ionization technique suitable for analyzing a wide range of molecules, mainly with high masses [1]. The simplicity of sample preparation, rapid spectra acquisition, high sensitivity, and relative tolerance to impurities make MALDI TOF (Time-of-Flight) attractive in various scientific fields. It is helpful for high-throughput direct detection of various analytes with analysis times ∼ 1 s per sample and nanoliter volumes. However, it has still been rarely used for small mass inorganic compounds. Most matrices have molecular weights below 300 Da (usually around 200 Da); therefore, the detector becomes saturated in the low mass range. Due to ion saturation and matrix interference, compounds with a mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) less than 500 are not easily analyzed using MALDI TOF MS. In recent years, various techniques have been tested to detect compounds below 500 m/z [2–4]. This area of research is growing, with more researchers testing the various organic and inorganic compounds to reduce the background of the MALDI matrix and expand the mass detection range. The latest MALDI TOF MS research was highlighted by combining the application examples in metal salts and metal complexes analysis [5–8]. Metal elements could be detected, but the oxidation states of the metals could be observed from the mass spectra when suitable chelating agents were used [9]. This method was also successfully applied to detect metal elements in a crystalline stone (Mineral objection) [5]. Mass spectrometric methods also provide a unique experimental tool for investigating the electronic and geometric properties and observing the unique chemistry in different sized metal clusters due to a wide detection range of TOF instruments [10–13].

Although there are many environmentally significant heavy metals, we selected cadmium as a model system for several reasons. For mass spectrometry experiments, the single oxidation state of cadmium (Cd2+) simplifies analysis and can help establish the model system. Cd2+can interact with a great variety of molecules due to its coordination flexibility. Steric interactions influence the coordination modes of cadmium. Increased interest surrounding Cd-coordination chemistry has yielded varying and complex structures. Cadmium acetate complexes are of great interest for studies of supramolecular chemistry as precursors for metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) and clusters with unique properties [14,15]. The variations of the atom types building up the clusters and MOFs, as well as their size, offer various possibilities to engineer their properties for specific technological and scientific demands in industrial applications such as adsorption, catalysis, gas storage, and more recently for biomedical purposes, such as exploiting the porosity of the material for drug delivery systems [16]. Cadmium ions also participate in biological systems, most often as toxins originating from heavy metal pollutants [17], further increasing the interest surrounding its various coordination chemistry.

In this contribution, we investigate the correlation of physicochemical properties of the Cd/matrix system with the detectability of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O (-OAc, CH3COO–), including its isotopic distribution, cluster formation, and reactivity with matrices by LDI and MALDI approach. We have used five traditional MALDI matrices, α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA), 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB), anthracene-1,8,9-triol (AT), 9-aminoacridine (9AA), and 5, 10, 15, 20-tetrakis (pentafluorophenyl) porphyrin (F20TPP). The selection of these matrix compounds ensured a range of molar masses, pKa values, and different functional groups. Possible dependence behind the matrix and analyte-matrix adducts parameters and LOD in different matrices are discussed. Along with (MA)LDI, we applied spectroscopic ellipsometry (SE) and theoretical (DFT) studies to explain obtained results.

Cadmium acetate dihydrate, Cd(OAc)2·2H2O, 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid (DHB), α-cyano-hydroxycinnamic acid (CHCA), anthracene-1,8,9-triol (dithranol, DT), 9-aminoacridine (9-AA), and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(pentafluorophenyl)porphyrin (F20TPP) were used as matrices. All reagents and solvents (acetone, methanol, and chloroform) used for the matrices and Cd(OAc)2·2H2O were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Taufkirchen, Germany) at the highest commercially available purity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples for mass spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: Cd(OAc)2·2H2O was dissolved in methanol to 1 mg/mL (stock solution). For the LDI-TOF MS analyses, a small volume (0.5 μL) of 0.5 mg/mL Cd(OAc)2·2H2O was applied onto the sample plate and left to dry at room temperature. Matrix solutions were freshly prepared in the following concentrations: DHB 77 mg/mL in acetone, CHCA 10 mg/mL in acetone, DT 10 mg/mL in acetone, 9-AA 15 mg/mL in acetone, and F20TPP 10 mg/mL in chloroform. Ten μL of the analyte stock solution and the same volume of matrices solutions were vigorously mixed in a tube, and 0.5 μL of the mixed solution was applied onto a stainless steel MALDI target plate and left to dry at room temperature. Each sample was applied in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solution of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O (4 mg/mL) was prepared in methanol, and serial dilutions were performed in the range 0.25–4.00 mg/mL. Samples were mixed with each matrix as described above so that the final concentrations of analyte were in the range 0.125–2.000 mg/mL, and the corresponding mass spectra were acquired. Analyses were done in triplicates. First, we calculated S/N values for each peak (S/N ≥ 3) from the corresponding cadmium acetate in all adducts and cluster forms in the spectrum. These values were then averaged. This approach has been used earlier as a measure of lysophosphatide concentration [18]. Next, a curve of response (mean S/N values) vs. concentrations is constructed, and the slope was determined. The LOD of Cd-related signals in different matrices was calculated with the generated data as LOD = 3.3(σ/s) where σ is the standard deviation of the response, and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The value σ was determined based on the standard deviation of the regression line.

The MALDI TOF MS measurements were performed with an Autoflex maX mass spectrometer (Bruker Daltonics, Bremen, Germany) equipped with a 355 nm N2 laser with the maximum repetition rate of 2 kHz, capable of executing both linear and reflector modes. The reflector detector acquires the negative ion spectra with delayed extraction conditions and without a low mass gate. Each spectrum represents the average of 1000 single laser shots. The accelerating voltage was held at 20 kV and the delay time at 100 ns for all experiments. The laser fluence was kept constant in the range from 13% to 23% above the threshold, depending on the matrix used (20% - DHB, 15% - CHCA, 21% − 9-AA, 23% - DT, and 13% - F20TPP) to optimize the S/N. The instrument software determined the peak intensities and S/N values, Flex analysis 2.2 (Bruker Daltonics). Cd(OAc)2·2H2O was ionized using laser emitting at 355 nm either (i) via laser desorption ionization (LDI) or (ii) with the assistance of different organic matrices (MALDI mode) commonly used in MALDI TOF MS.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA, Single-factor) has been used to compare the means of two groups: experimentally obtained isotopic distribution with the theoretic one.

The SE measurements were done using HORIBA Jobin Yvon, monochromatic device iHR-320 at room temperature, under the incident angle of 70° in the wavelength range from 200 to 2200 nm (photon energy range from 0.6 to 4.8 eV). Fitting the model to the experimental curve was estimated using the double Tauc-Lorentz (DTL) model, with the analytical parameters which are simultaneously determined through the iterative process change until the error function obtains the minimum value:
(1a)
χ 2
=
min
∑
i = 1
n
Ψ
teor
-
Ψ
eksp
i 2
Γ
Ψ , i
+
Δ
teor

Δ
eksp
i 2
Γ
Δ , i

In the above equation (1a), n is the total number of recorded points (Ψ exp , Δ exp ). At the same time (Ψ theor , Δ theor ) are experimental values of ellipsometric angles and corresponding values calculated from the model using a parameter Γ Δ,i for calculation of the experimental error. All calculations were performed using the DeltaPsi 2 software package, an integral part of the device. All the samples are prepared the same way as for the MALDI TOF MS measurements.

Vertical excitation (VE) energies and ionization potentials (IP) of the species studied in this work are obtained from the minimum energy-optimized geometries of each species. The calculations have been performed with the density functional theory (DFT) with the M05-2X functional [19] corrected by the empirical dispersion correction of Grimme [20,21] and in conjunction with the def2-SVPP basis sets [22,23] as they are implemented in the Gaussian program [24]. This methodology has already been successfully applied to recent works [25–28] and has been selected to ensure actual results [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: LDI TOF mass spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O
ce:section.ce:para: The negative ion LDI mass spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O is presented in Fig. 1 , and from m/z 100 to 2000, we detected many ions. According to the m/z values, identified signals are indicated, and their identities are given in Table 1 . These peaks mainly originated from cluster formation, with the general formula [Cd×(OAc)2×+1]− (×=1–8). Some ions result from the addition of methanol or/and water molecules to the coordination sphere, whereas minor products stem from the loss of CO or CO2 from acetate (Table 1). Similar clusters of the type [Cd×L2×+1]- (×=1–8, L = NO3 −-, −OAc, or Cl−) were detected in ESI MS negative ion mode from electrosprayed solutions of cadmium salts (Cd(NO3)2, Cd(OAc)2 and CdCl2) [31]. The most likely structure of detected clusters is represented in Fig. S1.

The [Cd2(AcO)5 + 3H2O]− ion at m/z 573.8 was the most abundant, indicating the stability of the core cluster, [Cd2(AcO)5]− (Fig. 1). This anionic dimer is formed after adding the acetate ligand to the neutral dimer molecule Cd2(OAc)4.

Matrix-free approach for the Cd(OAc)2·2H2O detection was possible since this salt absorbs at the wavelength of the laser used for ionization (λ = 355 nm). In Fig. 1(b), the extinction coefficient (k) of a thin film made from Cd(OAc)2·2H2O, in the same manner as for MALDI TOF MS, is presented in dependence on the wavelength. There are several maxima of k in the measured wavelength range. Among them, one is near λ = 355 nm. Since k is directly proportional to the absorption coefficient, one can only state that tested salt absorbs at 355 nm.

Cd(OAc)3 −, formed by the addition of the acetate ion to the molecule, is also detected in LDI spectrum, and it can be clearly distinguished from other signals (Fig. 2 ). Furthermore, the isotopic pattern of Cd(OAc)3 − almost perfectly matches the theoretical isotope distribution of the same ion (Fig. 2- upper spectrum is theoretical, and lower one is an experimental). Mean values of 5 measurements with standard deviations are presented together with theoretical values calculated by Selket software and indicated in Table 2 inserted in Fig. 2. Generally, the experimental data deviate slightly from the theoretical isotopic distribution (P-value = 0.954132, F = 0.003449505, which means no statistical differences from theoretical values).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MALDI TOF MS spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O
ce:section.ce:para: The matrix-free approach is helpful for the generation and direct characterization of metal clusters. However, numerous analyte-cluster-related signals might seriously hamper the analysis of analyte ions and complicate the mass spectrum (Fig. 1). Cluster avoidance is the primary reason why a significant excess of the matrix over the analyte is usually used. Despite designing the most suitable matrix for each compound class, finding a helpful matrix is usually a “trial and error” process. A key aspect for the further development of MALDI MS is a better understanding of the working principles of MALDI matrices. A perfect matrix should isolate the generated ions and prevent the generation of analyte clusters, for instance, dimer and/or oligomer formation [32].

Each of the five different matrix compounds used for the MALDI detection of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O were selected for this study, based on the optical properties, structural diversity, and different acidity, and their signals in the negative mode are identified in our previous work [7]. The structures of the matrices with the corresponding masses and some important matrix features are given in Supp Info.

In the negative ion mass spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O acquired with DHB (Fig. 3 (a)), several signals can be assigned to the analyte-DHB matrix adducts (Cf. Table 3 for peak identity). The most prominent Cd-related signal in the spectrum originated from [CdDHB3-3H]− (m/z 573.2), which is formed by the substitution of three acetate groups from [Cd(OAc)3]−with the matrix anions. Besides monomeric species, we detected oligonuclear adducts with two or three Cd metal centers (Table 3), in which DHB serves for bridging of metals. These adducts were detected at higher m/z values (over m/z 600) and exhibited a relatively low signal intensity (Fig. 3(a)). DHB, as an aromatic compound with three active donor groups (i.e., carboxyl group, hydroxyl groups at positions 2 and 5 of the benzene ring). Thus, 2,5-DHB can be a monodentate, bidentate, or bridging ligand for metal ions capable of forming highly stable charge transfer (CT) complexes, and cadmium is one of them. Besides the analyte-DHB matrix adducts, the signal which arises from the [Cd(OAc)3]− (m/z 289.4) was also detected in the MALDI TOF mass spectrum acquired with the DHB matrix, although with much lower intensity compared to the [CdDHB3-3H]−.

Similar to the spectrum obtained with DHB, in the spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O acquired with the CHCA matrix (Fig. 3(b)), signals that arise from the analyte/matrix adducts dominated the spectrum. The two dominant ions were assigned as [CdCHCA2-2H-2CO2]·− (m/z 401.0) and [CdCHCA3-3H]− (m/z 677.9), and other signals can be assigned to the Cd-CHCA adducts (Table 3). These adducts are formed by the interaction of the Cd2+ cation with the carboxyl group/s or π electrons of the aromatic ring of the CHCA by making “sandwich” structures with different masses [33]. In the spectra acquired with CHCA, no oligonuclear adducts are detected.

Most traditional MALDI matrices are acidic (e.g., DHB and CHCA), but 9AA is a strong base (pKa 9.99) [34]. 9AA assists in generating negative ions by the abstraction of a labile proton from the analyte molecule. The spectrum of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O in the 9AA matrix (Fig. 3(c)) contains signals originated from the cadmium acetate ions and dimer clusters (Fig. 3(c), Table 3). The dominant ions in these mass spectra correspond to [Cd(OAc)3]− (m/z 289.4) and [Cd(OAc)3 + 2H2O]−(m/z 325.4). In addition, there is also the ion generated by the methanol and water molecules' addition into the coordination sphere accompanied with the loss of CO from acetate [Cd(OAc)3-CO + MeOH + H2O]− (m/z 311.1). Only a few signals can be assigned to the analyte-matrix adducts or Cd-related dimmers (Cf. Table 3 for signal identity).

Mixing AT matrix with Cd(OAc)2·2H2O solution results in a color change from yellow to orange, but MALDI of this mixture does not yield Cd-related signals in the negative ion mode (Fig. 3(d)), which is expected due to the absence of acidic proton. On the other hand, color change suggests that AT underwent structural changes, which affect ionization ability. This change can be related to the oxidation catalyzed by metal ions, as observed previously [35,36]. Therefore, Cd(II) is most likely reduced to Cd(0), which was impossible to detect.

Compared to the mass spectra obtained with the other four matrices, the mass spectrum of the analyte acquired in F20TPP was straightforward with only a few background signals. F20TPP matrix yields one signal, which does not interfere with the Cd-related ions detectable in MALDI mass spectrum (Fig. 3(e)). These ions, detectable at the higher m/z values, result from the metalation of the porphyrin ring, a tetradentate ligand. A single matrix signal, indicated by an asterisk, does not interfere with the additional three signals that arise from the matrix-analyte adducts. None of the signals was detected in the low mass range (Table 1). It is well known that divalent and trivalent metal ions can be incorporated into the protoporphyrin cavity, leading to very stable inner complexes [37]. Cd2+ can coordinate with four nitrogen atoms in the porphyrin ring of an F20TPP matrix molecule (for F20TPP structure, see Table S1) to give Cd-matrix complex. Since the coordination of a metal ion with a porphyrin depends mainly on the space within the porphyrin core (approximate radius = 75–90 pm), large metal ions, such as Cd2+, cannot fit into the porphyrin nucleus and only sits on top of the molecule [38]. Therefore, F20TPP absorbs the laser energy and acts as a matrix and forms F20TPP -analyte adduct, and shifts the low mass analyte to a higher mass region through conjugation reaction. This process resulted in the elimination of the interference peaks in the low mass region. This matrix was successfully employed earlier to analyze several small mass compounds such as alkylphenol ethoxylates, the fatty acid composition of vegetable oils, detection of sugars, ascorbic acid, citric acid, sodium-benzoate, FeCl3, and also for the quantification of several pharmaceutically-relevant molecules [2,7,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: LODs of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O in the tested matrices
ce:section.ce:para: LODs for each combination of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O/matrix were defined as the lowest Cd concentration that MALDI MS can reliably measure. The LODs for Cd(OAc)2·2H2O determined in DHB, CHCA, F20TPP and 9AA, were 0.1111 mg/mL, 0.1553 mg/mL, 0.4651 mg/mL, and 0.9911 mg/mL, respectively, indicating that the most sensitive detection of Cd was in DHB matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation of physicochemical properties of matrices with LOD of Cd-ions
ce:section.ce:para: Defining the relationship between the various physicochemical properties of a matrix compound and the LODs for analyte detection is a long-standing task in MALDI TOF MS. This is not easy because of a complex interaction between various factors during desorption and ionization.

The low melting point (mp) of the matrix assists the vaporization process from the MALDI plate because this enhances the ionization through the phase transition. Phase transition increases entropy and favors ionization. Furthermore, to achieve the efficient ionization of analytes, low ionization energies and refractive index (RI) of the matrix material are favorable [40], and matrices with a low ionization potential (IP) are more readily ionized, have a higher tendency to produce free electrons and interact with neutral molecules of the analyte [41]. Additionally, low IP facilitates the production of radical molecular ions [42], and the refractive index defines how much a ray of light changes direction during the entering a matrix material. The increasing refractive index corresponds to the decreasing speed of light in the material. According to some models, the ion yield also depends on the pK a [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ionization potential, pKa, and mp of matrices and LOD
ce:section.ce:para: In the next step of experiments, we checked the potential correlation between the pKa, IP, RI, and mp of matrix and LOD to detect Cd-related signals, and the results are given in Fig. 4 . No correlation could be established between indicated properties and the LOD of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O, not even for mp. Tested matrices have similar mp values, Fig. 4(b), with the lowest one for DHB, which can be related to the lowest LOD for Cd(OAc)2·2H2O. However, even the difference in mp values between DHB and CHCA are significant, the LOD between DHB and CHCA is rather small (0.1111 vs. 0.1553 mg/mL). At the same time, the differences in LOD for matrices with similar mp (i.e. CHCA, F20TPP and 9AA) are greater (0.1553 vs 0.4651 vs 0.9911 mg/mL). Therefore, another factor appears to be significantly influencing the LOD value. Lack of unambiguous correlation of matrix parameters and LODs could be explained by the high affinity between the analyte molecule and the matrices to form analyte-matrix adducts, modifying matrix features (IP, RI and etc) and affecting matrix performances.

Calculations have been performed with the DFT to obtained IP and VE energies of the most abundant ions detected in the mass spectra of all tested matrices to explain the effect of matrix/analyte complexes formation and analyte detectability. Results are given in Table 4 . Formation of matrix complexes with Cd(OAc)-species lowers IP and detects negative ion mode MALDI mass spectra easier, thus increasing the ion yield. Compared with LDI mode, the complexes with organic matrices also have lower IP values, particularly complex generated with 9AA (Table 4). The value of oscillator strength, which is 0.000, indicates easy ionization and detectability in MALDI mass spectra. An increase in the sensitivity of metal-ions detection with organic matrices compared to LDI mode was also obtained to detect FeCl3 in various matrices [7]. VE energies of ions detected in LDI and MALDI mass spectra of Cd(OAc)2·2H2O (expressed in eV, Table 4) also suggest much lower energies required for ionization of organic/metals ions in comparison to Cd-ions detected in the LDI mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the matrix optical properties on the Cd(OAc)2·2H2O LODs studied by SE
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of matrices are the critical factor affecting ion formation and desorption. The matrix should absorb the laser energy, which heats matrix/analyte co-crystals resulting in the sublimation and further ionization [44]. The interplay between matrix optical absorption and laser wavelength strongly determines MALDI MS performances to analyze a specific analyte [44,45]. Optical properties are usually measured from solutions [46,47], but the absorption properties of MALDI matrices should be determined in the solid state. We used SE to assess the properties of the analyte, all matrices, and analyte/matrix combinations in a solid-state (Fig. 1(b) and Fig. 3(f-j)).

Interference fringes are observed in the SE spectra of Cd(OAc) × 2H2O in methanol (Fig. 1(b)), indicating layers of a compound stacked along. It is already shown that cadmium acetate hydrate in alcohol solution could form stacked layers and that intermolecular hydrogen bonds ensure cohesion between them. Poul et al. [48] reported the crystal structure of a cadmium hydroxy-carboxylate compound, which is bi-dimensional and built up of Cd5(OH)(H2O)3(CH3CO2)9 layers. Therefore, we can state that the clusters built up from units detected in MALDI TOF mass spectra form layers, as can be observed by SE.

Correlation between higher absorption coefficient and better matrix performances was already shown by Robinson et al. [44]. In our case, the ordering of LODs generally did not reflect the absorption ability of matrices at the wavelength of the laser used (km355 ) (Fig. 5). Deviations from the trend were the most prominent in the case of AT. AT has the third highest value of km355 , right after CHCA, and none of the signals originated from the analyte molecule have not been detected. According to the MALDI MS spectrum (Fig. 3(d)), only matrix signals were detected. Therefore, the optical properties of the matrix only do not determine LOD of Cd-ions. On the other hand, matrix-analyte adducts' formation may change the absorption coefficients of the matrices. After the addition of Cd(OAc) × 2H2O to AT matrix, total suppression of the signal in the SE spectrum could be observed (Fig. 3 (i)) and a decrease of kma355 from 2.86 to zero, which implies that the matrix is not capable of absorbing energy from the laser anymore. The decrease of kma355 explains the absence of the signals from analyte molecules in the MALDI MS spectrum recorded with this matrix. Significant changes in km355 values and shape-shift of the SE spectra of matrices are observed after the addition of the analyte into the matrices (Fig. 3(f-j), red lines), due to the formation of matrix-analyte adducts that are already detected and assigned by MALDI TOF MS (Fig. 3(a-e)., Table 3.) The km355 values after the addition of the analyte molecules are marked as kma355 and given in Table 5, Fig. 5. kma355 values had the following order DHB > CHCA > F20TPP > 9AA > AT, which was in correlation with the corresponding LODs values of the matrices (Fig. 5). Generally, the higher the km355 value is, the higher is the ability of the given matrix or formed matrix/analyte adduct to absorb the laser's energy, produce higher ion yield, and therefore had higher sensitivity.

In conclusion, we have demonstrated that (MA)LDI MS approach could be employed to analyze Cd(OAc)2·2H2O and that optical properties of the products of interactions with matrix in combination with vertical excitation properties of the products determine the sensitivity of detection of Cd-related ions. In contrast, other physicochemical properties do not significantly affect ion yield. Correlation trends between optical properties of matrix (analyte-matrix adduct) and corresponding LOD for the analyte reveal that the LOD of the analyte decreases with an increasing absorption coefficient of the matrix-analyte adduct is demonstrated. Cd(OAc)2·2H2O forms adducts with almost all tested matrices, illustrating cadmium coordination chemistry's complex and flexible nature. MALDI analysis of negative ions can thus provide additional information about metal cluster structure and the reactivity with different reagents, including small organic molecules, which can serve for the complexation of toxic Cd-based compounds and their elimination from the environment direct detection.

The results presented in this paper obtained by a combination of theory and experiments can give the initial insight into the MALDI performance to detect a specific analyte, provide a better platform for the experimental verification of theoretical models, and rational selection of matrix compounds.

Maja D. Nešić: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Manuel Algarra: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Juan Soto: Formal analysis, Investigation. Miloš Nenadović: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Iva Popović: Software, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Marijana Petković: Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Experiments at MALDI TOF MS were performed at CQM-Madeira Chemistry Research Centre, University of Madeira, Funchal, Portugal, by M. ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2021.115463.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The suitability of different DHB isomers as matrices for the MALDI-TOF MS analysis of phospholipids: which isomer for what purpose?

Waschipky, The suitability of different DHB isomers as matrices for the MALDI-TOF MS analysis of phospholipids: which isomer for what purpose?, Eur Biophys J. 36 (2007) 517–527. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00249-006-0090-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: [Cd5(OH)(H2O)3][CH3CO2]9, a cadmium hydroxy-acetate compound with a layered network

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Poul, M. Fakhfakh, M. Taibi, N. Jouini, P. Herson, F. Fiévet, [Cd5(OH)(H2O)3][CH3CO2]9 , a cadmium hydroxy-acetate compound with a layered network, J Chem Crystallogr. 35 (2005) 285–291. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10870-005-1439-x.
